graded_decisions.from_session: unknown (predates 30-day order history; no decision log entry exists)
graded_decisions.news_since: Stock fell ~17-20% YTD into late June on worries that Microsoft's ~$190B FY26 AI capex (driven by soaring memory costs) was outpacing returns, then rallied +10.6% in the review window (close $352.42 on 6/25 to $389.79 on 7/2) as institutional money rotated out of AI chip/hardware names into AI software leaders (The Motley Fool, 'Why Microsoft Stock Surged Today,' 7/1/2026; Invezz, 7/1/2026).
graded_decisions.comment: No original entry reasoning exists in the decision log, so the thesis can't be checked against what was actually claimed. Azure (+40%) and Cloud (+29%) growth support the stock being oversold, but this week's bounce looks like a sector-rotation trade (chips->software) ahead of the late-July earnings print, not confirmed new fundamentals. Still down 8.2% since entry -- treat as unresolved until earnings, not validated.

graded_decisions.from_session: unknown (predates 30-day order history; no decision log entry exists)
graded_decisions.news_since: Jensen Huang conceded Nvidia has 'largely conceded' China's advanced AI chip market to Huawei (near-zero China data-center revenue), and B200 GPU lease pricing fell from $6.11/hr to $4.22/hr between 5/30 and 6/21, signaling margin pressure from rising competition -- this despite a record ~$82B revenue quarter (TradingKey, 6/25/2026; Yahoo Finance, June 2026).
graded_decisions.comment: This is a structural headwind, not sentiment noise: lost China market share plus falling GPU lease rates both compress the long-term margin/growth story. Flat this week (-0.7%) looks like stabilization, not reversal. Largest single-name position (15 shares, $2,922 market value) carrying the account's biggest unrealized loss -- needs an explicit invalidation level, not a 'hold and hope for earnings' default.

graded_decisions.news_since: Nasdaq-100 underperformed the S&P 500 this week as investors rotated out of the 80%+ H1 semiconductor/AI rally (Micron, Sandisk, Applied Materials, Intel, Marvell all -9-10% that week) into broader cyclicals, pushing the Dow to records -- described as a healthy 'Great Rotation' (TheStreet, 7/1/2026) -- compounded by a weak June jobs report (+57K vs. consensus, CNBC, 7/2/2026) that favored defensive/broad-market positioning over high-beta tech.
graded_decisions.comment: Down since the 6/16 entry and lagging SPY by ~2pp this week specifically because of the tech-to-cyclicals rotation. Research suggests this reads as short-term profit-taking after an extraordinary H1 semis rally, not a durable leadership change -- Micron is still +260% YTD even after the pullback. Worth monitoring for a second consecutive down week before treating it as more than rotation.

graded_decisions.from_session: unknown (predates 30-day order history; no decision log entry exists)
graded_decisions.news_since: S&P 500/Dow hit record highs this week as capital rotated out of overheated AI/semiconductor names into broader, more cyclical exposure, and a weak June jobs report (+57K vs. consensus) reinforced a shift toward defensive/broad-market positioning (TheStreet, 7/1/2026; CNBC, 7/2/2026).
graded_decisions.comment: Simple broad-market exposure was the best-performing single holding this week for exactly the reason QQQ lagged -- it captured the rotation into cyclicals/broad market rather than fighting it. Only 3 shares (10.9% of account) -- position is too small to meaningfully offset the concentrated AI/tech risk elsewhere in the book.

patterns: CRITICAL -- the decision journal (journal/decisions.jsonl) has zero entries, ever, despite the account holding 4 live positions and at least one confirmed fill (QQQ, 2026-06-16). The daily routine is not writing decisions/reasoning to the log the README specifies, which means this review has no session-level reasoning to grade against and the 'read prior decisions at session start' feedback loop the whole system depends on is currently non-functional.

No trades occurred in the past 7 days (alpaca orders --hours 168 returned empty) and only 2 fills exist in the past 30 days (both 2026-06-16: QQQ buy, AAPL sell). The book has been static through a genuinely volatile 2.5-week stretch -- NVDA absorbed a fundamental negative catalyst (China GPU market ceded to Huawei) and MSFT round-tripped -17% then +10.6% -- with no trims, adds, or stop-outs in response.

The basket is concentrated in correlated AI/tech-adjacent exposure: MSFT + NVDA + QQQ together are ~65% of long market value ($6,231.72 of $8,466.06), and all three carry unrealized losses since entry while the lone broad-market position (SPY) is the only winner. The 'diversified basket' is effectively one leveraged bet on AI/tech sentiment plus a small market hedge.
suggested_adjustments: Fix the decision journal first: every session (including 'hold'/'skip' days) must append an entry to journal/decisions.jsonl per the README schema -- symbol, action, reasoning, research_cited. Until this happens, no future weekly review can actually grade decisions against stated theses, only against price action after the fact.

Treat NVDA's China-market-share loss and falling GPU lease pricing as a fundamental thesis break, not noise -- define an explicit invalidation/stop level for the position (currently -10.25% and the account's largest single-name exposure) rather than defaulting to hold.

Cap combined AI-hardware + AI-software + tech-heavy-ETF exposure (NVDA + MSFT + QQQ) at a target % of equity before adding new correlated longs -- right now that bucket is ~67% of equity and all three names sold off together in June for related reasons (AI capex/margin worries).

Don't treat a single sharp reversal week as thesis confirmation without corroboration: MSFT's +10.6% bounce is attributed by sources to sector rotation ahead of its late-July earnings print, not new fundamentals -- require 2+ outlets and/or the actual earnings print before upgrading the thesis from 'oversold bounce' to 'trend resumed.'
summary: No decisions were logged this week (journal/decisions.jsonl is empty) and no trades were placed (last fill 2026-06-16) -- the four open positions (MSFT, NVDA, QQQ, SPY) simply rode the week's price action. Portfolio +1.32% vs SPY +1.57%, VT +0.84%, SSO +2.94% (SSO's 2x-vs-SPY relationship held roughly: 2.94% vs. 2x1.57%=3.14%, a small daily-reset drag). NVDA's decline looks structurally driven (China share loss, margin compression) and is the account's biggest problem position; QQQ's weekly lag looks like short-term sector rotation rather than a trend break; SPY and MSFT's bounce were the bright spots. The single biggest issue this week isn't any individual trade -- it's that the decision log the whole review process depends on is empty, so there is nothing to actually grade against stated reasoning.

graded_decisions.news_since: Azure/AI thesis has not been fundamentally refuted (Azure +40% YoY held up across two subsequent earnings prints, AI ARR +123% YoY), but the stock round-tripped from $425 entry to a $460 peak (6/1) down to a $352 trough (6/25) on $190B AI-capex/FCF-compression fears, then partly recovered to $390 on a chips-to-software rotation ahead of late-July earnings.
graded_decisions.comment: PROCESS FAILURE, not a thesis failure: the 5/19 entry explicitly set 'stock closes below $400' as the invalidation level. MSFT closed below $400 on 2026-06-10 and kept falling to $352 -- that trigger was never revisited in any subsequent session. On 6/17 the routine set a NEW self-imposed trigger ('watch $380 for break-below trim'); MSFT closed at $365 on 6/24 and $352 on 6/25, decisively through that level too, and still no trim happened. Research quality each day was genuinely good (fresh, specific data, not boilerplate) -- the gap is between research and enforcement.

graded_decisions.news_since: Concentration was actively and correctly managed early (trimmed from ~45% to ~34% of equity across 5/28 and 6/2 on post-earnings/post-COMPUTEX strength). But from early June onward, Nvidia conceded China's advanced AI chip market to Huawei and B200 GPU lease pricing fell ~31% ($6.11->$4.22/hr, 5/30-6/21), signaling real margin compression -- despite another record ~$82B revenue quarter.
graded_decisions.comment: Good sizing discipline early, but no further de-risking once the thesis shifted from 'earnings momentum' to 'structural China/margin erosion' in June. Largest single-name position in the book; still the account's biggest loser by percentage terms.

graded_decisions.news_since: Entered on an Iran/Strait-of-Hormuz oil-supply-shock thesis (5/21). Six days later, the IEA's May report showed global oil demand contracting (-2.4 mb/d YoY in Q2) and a US-Iran deal reduced the war premium -- thesis invalidated almost immediately.
graded_decisions.comment: Good exit discipline (cut it fast rather than holding through hope) even though the entry itself was a quick loser. XLE kept falling after the exit too (to $53.24 by 7/2, another -6.7%), so the exit timing was correct. On 6/30 the routine considered re-entering XLE citing 'Brent ~$105/bbl' and 'XLE +38.2% YTD,' but actual XLE closes show a steady decline the entire period with no such spike -- worth checking why that price claim didn't match the ETF's real chart before it nearly triggered a re-entry.

graded_decisions.news_since: Built an 8-share position over two weeks entirely on anticipation of the June 8 WWDC AI/Siri reveal, citing Street-high analyst price targets ($400 Wedbush, $380 BofA) and 'agentic AI' upside. WWDC delivered a Siri overhaul that arrived roughly two years late and was blocked by EU and China regulation across ~35% of iPhone shipments -- a clear disappointment.
graded_decisions.comment: Textbook buy-the-rumor-sell-the-news: added into a well-telegraphed catalyst that was already reflected in raised price targets, then closed at a loss when the actual event undershot the hype baked into those targets.

graded_decisions.news_since: Nasdaq-100 underperformed the S&P 500 in the most recent week on a 'Great Rotation' out of semis/AI-hardware profit-taking into cyclicals, compounded by a weak June jobs print (+57K vs. consensus). Sources read this as short-term rotation, not a durable leadership change.
graded_decisions.comment: This is the second QQQ entry -- the first 1-share lot (bought 5/28 at ~$735.64) was sold 6/3 at ~$744.21 (a small +1.2% gain) specifically to fund more AAPL, which then lost 4.4% on the WWDC bust. Net effect of that rotation: gave up a working diversifier to add to a losing conviction bet. The current 3-share lot (bought 6/16 for the same 'diversify away from single-name AI risk' reason) is down 4.1% since entry, mostly from being bought right before this rotation-driven pullback.

graded_decisions.news_since: Broad market hit record highs this week on rotation into cyclicals and a weak jobs report favoring defensive positioning; no session-level research was ever logged for SPY specifically because it required none.
graded_decisions.comment: The one position with zero active management and zero research citations across 45 sessions is the single best performer in the book, and it beat every actively-managed name. Only 3 shares (~11% of equity) -- too small to have offset the losses elsewhere.

patterns: INVALIDATION LEVELS ARE SET BUT NEVER ENFORCED. MSFT's 5/19 entry set 'stock closes below $400' as its stop; MSFT closed below $400 on 6/10 and fell to $352 by 6/25 without a single session flagging the breach. A second, self-imposed trigger set on 6/17 ('$380 break-below = trim') was breached on 6/24-6/25 and also never acted on. This is the single most concrete, fixable failure in the whole history: research is genuinely updated with fresh data every session, but the stated triggers from prior sessions are never checked against current price before deciding to hold again.

Catalyst-anticipation trades lose money twice in the same way: AAPL was built up over two weeks purely ahead of WWDC on Street-high analyst targets, then closed at a loss when the event disappointed (-4.4%). XLE was bought on an Iran/Hormuz war-shock narrative and invalidated within 6 days by IEA demand data (-5.2%). Both are 'buy the well-telegraphed catalyst, thesis already priced in, event undershoots' failures.

Whipsaw churn between QQQ and AAPL: bought 1 QQQ (5/28), sold it 6 days later to fund more AAPL (6/3), then closed all of AAPL and bought QQQ again (6/16) -- three moves between the same two vehicles in three weeks, netting a modest loss overall once the AAPL leg is included.

Concentration risk was well-managed early (NVDA trimmed from ~45% to ~34% of equity across two sessions in late May) but that discipline was never reapplied after 6/2, even as new fundamental risks emerged on both MSFT (capex/FCF compression) and NVDA (China market exit) through June.

The single unmanaged, un-researched position (SPY, held flat for 45 sessions) outperformed every actively-managed name and the whole book. The portfolio's entire active management layer has subtracted value relative to just holding the passive core.
suggested_adjustments: Make invalidation checks mechanical, not narrative: every session must explicitly compare current price to any invalidation/trigger level set in a prior session for each open position, and if breached, the default action is trim/close -- not a fresh 'thesis still intact' paragraph. This alone would have caught both MSFT breaches.
If a position is down >15% from entry AND has breached its own stated trigger, require an actual size reduction before the position is allowed to just 'hold' again in the next session.

Discount well-telegraphed catalysts (WWDC, COMPUTEX, earnings) that are already reflected in raised analyst price targets going into the event -- require the outcome to beat the re-priced expectation, not just occur, before sizing into it. This would have blocked the AAPL WWDC add.

Stop rotating capital between overlapping vehicles (QQQ vs. direct mega-cap names) to fund a different conviction trade -- pick single-name or broad-ETF exposure per thesis and hold it, rather than swapping mid-stream.
summary: Full-history review (2026-05-19 to 2026-07-03, 45 sessions -- the first time this data has been visible on main, having been recovered from 44 previously-orphaned branches). Portfolio is down -6.80% since the $10,000 inception while every passive benchmark is positive: SPY +1.51%, VT +2.29%, SSO +2.33% (SSO also shows a real leverage-decay gap here -- 2.33% actual vs. ~3.01% theoretical 2xSPY, a meaningful drag from six-plus weeks of choppy, directionless price action). Alpha vs. SPY is -8.31 points. The research quality underlying each day's decisions is genuinely good and specific, not boilerplate -- the failure is structural: stated invalidation levels (MSFT's $400 stop, then its own $380 trim-trigger) are set and then never checked again, two separate catalyst-anticipation trades (AAPL/WWDC, XLE/Hormuz) lost money in the same 'priced-in event disappoints' pattern, and the single passive, unmanaged position (SPY) quietly beat every actively-managed name in the book.

graded_decisions.news_since: Closed on stop discipline (the $400 trigger had been breached since 6/10, per the 07-03 review's own criticism), citing FCF compression, capex intensity, a UK cloud-licensing antitrust probe and 4,800 layoffs. Since the exit, MSFT rallied to $401.12 by 7/16 (Azure-share-gain and Frontier Co AI-unit news through early July) before easing to $392.15 on 7/17 -- pulled down by the broad chip/Netflix selloff, not fresh MSFT-specific news.
graded_decisions.comment: PROCESS WIN, TIMING MISS: this is exactly the fix the last two reviews demanded -- an overdue stop finally enforced instead of another 'thesis intact' narrative -- and that discipline is worth preserving even though hindsight shows the stock recovered above the $385 exit price (would be -7.8% held vs. -9.39% realized, a ~1.6pt cost). No re-entry signal yet; earnings 7/29 is the next real test.

graded_decisions.news_since: Closed same-day on a clean mechanical $700 stop breach amid a genuine, broad chip/AI-capex de-rating -- the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index down ~19% from its late-June record (worst week since March 2025), compounded by a Netflix Q3 guidance miss (-9.7%) broadening the selloff into general growth-tech. QQQ kept falling intraday to a $686.78 low and closed 7/17 at $693.79, below the exit price -- confirming the stop fired at the right time, not prematurely.
graded_decisions.comment: This is the cleanest process execution in the book's history: trigger set 7/14, monitored explicitly for narrowing cushion on 7/16, and closed in real time when breached on 7/17 rather than lagging a month like the original MSFT failure. Directly validates the last two reviews' top suggested fix.

graded_decisions.news_since: Mixed-to-neutral company news (Kyber NVL144 delay rumor denied, Vera Rubin confirmed in production, Goldman calls 21.7x forward P/E 'compelling', UAE export-license easing) was offset by a sector-wide de-rating: NVDA fell from $212.50 (7/15) to $202.94 (7/17) as TSMC's raised 2026 capex guidance ($60-64B, from $52-56B) was read by the market as margin/ROI risk rather than demand strength, and Michael Burry's short thesis (B200 lease-rate decay, hyperscaler depreciation-accounting concerns) persisted through the window, though unconfirmed by any new 13F.
graded_decisions.comment: Stop at $190 never threatened (position stayed 7.5-8% clear all window), so holding was mechanically correct. But concentration risk flagged in every prior review is still unaddressed: NVDA is now ~32% of equity and, with MSFT and QQQ both closed, is the single largest active risk position in the book with zero size reduction since the late-May trims.

graded_decisions.news_since: Added 1.5 shares on a confirmed SMA crossover plus a cooler-than-expected June CPI print (+3.5% YoY vs 3.8% expected). The crossover thesis was directionally right (SPY hit a fresh high of $754.92 the next day, 7/15) but the position was bought right before the same 7/16-7/17 chip/Netflix selloff that hit QQQ and NVDA pulled the broad market down too, giving back the gain and then some.
graded_decisions.comment: Blended across the full SPY position (4.5 sh, avg $738.93) the book is still +0.74% and remains the account's best-performing, least-managed holding overall -- the specific 7/14 add was simply bought one to two days ahead of a broad-market wobble, a minor timing miss on an otherwise sound thesis, not a thesis failure.

patterns: INVALIDATION ENFORCEMENT FINALLY WORKED -- twice. This is the headline result of the window: MSFT's overdue stop was finally closed (7/10) and, more importantly, QQQ's $700 stop was caught and closed the SAME DAY it breached (7/17), with the cushion explicitly flagged as narrowing the day before (7/16). This directly answers the #1 complaint raised in both prior weekly reviews (invalidation levels set but never enforced) and should be locked in as a permanent process rule rather than treated as a one-off.

First positive-alpha window on record: portfolio +0.51% vs SPY -0.05%, VT -0.96%, SSO -0.41% over 2026-07-02 to 2026-07-17 -- despite every graded decision this window carrying a negative or near-flat price outcome. The alpha came from risk management (cutting MSFT and QQQ before the worst of the 7/16-7/17 selloff), not from stock-picking -- worth recognizing explicitly since it's the opposite failure mode from the last two reviews.

CRITICAL, WORSENING: the orphaned-branch infrastructure bug flagged in the 07-03 and 07-10 reviews has gotten materially worse, not better. There are now 68 unmerged claude/* branches on origin (up from '40+' reported two reviews ago), each holding exactly one day's decision-journal entry that never auto-merged to main. The 07-15 and 07-16 sessions both had to spend part of their run manually 'recovering' stranded entries, and today's QQQ-close session (07-17) is itself still stranded on a branch (claude/adoring-mccarthy-6k9omr) as of this review -- it was only found by searching git history, not by reading main. If a future session skips the recovery step, the 'read prior decisions at session start' continuity this whole system depends on breaks silently again.

NVDA concentration risk is now the account's largest unmanaged exposure by default rather than by decision: with MSFT and QQQ both closed this window, NVDA (~32% of equity) is the only sizeable actively-managed position left, and no session has revisited its size independent of the $190 stop since the late-May trims -- the same gap flagged in the last two reviews persists unchanged.
suggested_adjustments: Escalate the branch/merge pipeline fix beyond the daily routine's reach: this is the third consecutive review flagging it, the branch count has grown ~70% since the last review (40+ -> 68), and today's own QQQ-close decision was still stranded off-main at review time. Recommend the account owner check whether the daily session's git workflow is actually configured to merge/push to main (not just commit to a fresh branch each run), since self-recovery is a symptom being repeatedly patched, not the cause being fixed.

Codify mechanical invalidation-checking as a standing, non-negotiable rule (not just this window's fix): every session must diff current price against every open position's most recently stated trigger before writing any 'hold' rationale, and default to trim/close on breach. It worked twice this window (MSFT, QQQ) -- keep it from regressing once the current losing streak on those names fades from memory.

Set an explicit NVDA sizing cap independent of the price stop: e.g. trim to <=25% of equity on the next relief rally of +3% or more, since three consecutive reviews have flagged NVDA's concentration and no session has acted on it outside the binary $190 stop.

Require a 2-day confirmation hold on SMA-crossover buy signals (SPY 7/14) before sizing in, rather than acting the same session the crossover is confirmed -- the 7/14 SPY add was bought into a fresh high right before a broad selloff; waiting even one session to confirm the crossover held would have cost little and avoided buying the local top.
summary: First positive-alpha window in this journal's history: portfolio +0.51% vs SPY -0.05%, VT -0.96%, SSO -0.41% (2026-07-02 to 2026-07-17), driven by risk management rather than stock-picking -- both MSFT (7/10) and QQQ (7/17) were closed on mechanically-enforced price stops, the exact fix the last two reviews demanded after finding stops set but never acted on. QQQ's close in particular was caught the same day it breached, ahead of a broader chip/AI-capex selloff (SOX -19% from its late-June record, Netflix guidance miss) that dragged NVDA and the rest of the tape down further on 7/16-7/17. The cost of that discipline is real but modest: MSFT would be showing -7.8% held vs. -9.39% realized. The account's remaining problems are structural, not tactical: NVDA is now ~32% of equity with no size reduction since May despite three straight reviews flagging it, and the orphaned-git-branch bug that strands daily journal entries off main has gotten worse, not better (68 unmerged branches now vs. 40+ two reviews ago) -- today's own QQQ-close decision was still off-main at review time and had to be recovered from a branch to grade this window at all.

graded_decisions.news_since: No fresh NVDA-specific negative since the trim; TSMC's Jul 15-16 beat-and-raise (capex to $60-64B, revenue growth guide >40%) was read bearishly for margins, then the market rotated into memory names (Micron, SK Hynix) over NVDA on Jul 23 despite Alphabet raising 2026 AI capex to $195-205B (Invezz, Yahoo Finance, Jul 23-24).
graded_decisions.comment: This is the first time in the book's history a concentration-cap rule was executed proactively -- into a relief rally, before a stop breach forced it -- exactly what the last three weekly reviews demanded (NVDA cut from ~32.8% to ~24.9% of equity). The 1.7% of upside given up on the trimmed shares since 07-20 is a small, acceptable cost for finally fixing unmanaged concentration risk that had persisted since May.

graded_decisions.news_since: Thesis catalyst is real and intensifying on the news: Brent topped $100/bbl on Jul 22-23 for the first time since May after Houthi rebels attacked two Saudi tankers (Encelia, Layla) in the Red Sea, opening a second front alongside the Strait of Hormuz strikes (Al Jazeera, CNBC, military.com, Jul 22-23). But Brent then retreated as much as 4.3% on Friday Jul 24 on the same tariff headwinds pressuring equities, and XLE itself has barely moved off the $60.26 entry despite the oil spike -- the equity hasn't confirmed the commodity move yet.
graded_decisions.comment: Good process on the entry itself: waited 5 sessions for a clean, multi-day-confirmed catalyst instead of chasing headlines, sized modestly (7.7% of equity), and it doubles as a hedge against the same oil/rate risk pressuring SPY. But XLE lagging a genuine +20%+ Brent move this hard, combined with Friday's sharp Brent pullback, means the position is unconfirmed by price action -- the next 1-2 sessions are the real test, not the entry itself.

graded_decisions.news_since: $190 hard stop never threatened all window (stayed 7-10% clear). This week's softness (NVDA -2.18% Jul 23 despite Alphabet's capex raise) was investors rotating into memory/foundry names (Micron +2.5%, SK Hynix +2.8%) rather than an NVDA-specific negative -- confirmed independently via search, matching what the daily routine cited.
graded_decisions.comment: Mechanically correct hold with the concentration cap now respected (~25% of equity, maintained since the 07-20 trim). Aug 26 FY2027 Q2 earnings remains the real unresolved catalyst; nothing this window changes that calculus.

graded_decisions.news_since: The $735 hard stop was not breached (closed 07-23 at $738.06, intraday low $735.25 -- within 11 cents of the stop) as the S&P fell 1.21% Jul 23 on Alphabet (-7%) and Tesla (-14.5%) capex-driven selloffs plus new Section 301 tariffs effective Jul 24 (Motley Fool, CNBC, Epoch Times).
graded_decisions.comment: Process was followed correctly (wait for an actual close-below breach, don't pre-empt), but the cushion sat under 1% for five consecutive sessions without ever widening, even on up days -- that is a real near-miss, not just a flagged risk. This is the account's largest position and best historical performer; a weekend gap-down before Monday's open would have blown through $735 with zero chance to react intraday.

patterns: Concentration-cap discipline finally generalized beyond reactive stop-outs: the 07-20 NVDA trim was the first time a review-mandated risk rule was executed proactively on a relief rally rather than only after a hard stop forced action (as with MSFT/QQQ in June-July). The same discipline generalized further on 07-21/07-22 when a brand-new AAPL long candidate with a live buy signal was declined purely on the pre-earnings-preview rule -- the lesson from the AAPL/WWDC and MSFT losses is now being applied prospectively to new names, not just retrospectively to existing ones.

Portfolio beat all three passive benchmarks this week (+0.74% vs SPY -0.39%, VT -0.19%, SSO -0.85%) for the second straight review window, but the mechanism is different from last time: last week's alpha came from stops cutting MSFT/QQQ ahead of a selloff; this week's alpha looks like it came mostly from being under-invested during Thursday's broad selloff -- the account is carrying ~32% cash ($2,993 of $9,433 equity, ~68% gross long exposure) versus a fully-invested benchmark. That cash buffer helped this week but would equally cap upside in a rally week; it should be recognized as a structural, not tactical, source of the outperformance.

SPY's stop cushion has been flagged as 'narrowing' in the daily journal for five consecutive sessions (07-20 through 07-24, from ~1.4% down to as tight as ~0.09% intraday) without ever triggering a size reduction ahead of the actual breach -- the routine is correctly not pre-empting on narrative, but a stop that stays this thin for this long through a genuinely deteriorating macro backdrop (tariffs, Mag7 capex selloff, rising FOMC hike odds) is a candidate for a partial, rules-based trim rather than an all-or-nothing binary stop.

The orphaned git-branch bug is now in its fourth consecutive weekly review with no fix: two more sessions (07-22, 07-23) were found stranded off main and had to be manually recovered on 07-24. Self-healing by the daily routine is masking, not solving, an infrastructure problem that has now caused four separate continuity gaps since early July.
suggested_adjustments: Add a rules-based partial-trim trigger for cushion decay, not just binary stops: if a position's stop cushion stays under 1% for 3+ consecutive sessions without widening (as SPY did 07-20 to 07-24), cut position size by a fixed fraction (e.g. 25-30%) rather than waiting for an outright close-below breach that a weekend gap could skip past entirely.

Treat the current ~32% cash buffer as an explicit, stated risk-budget decision going forward (target range, e.g. 15-30% of equity) rather than an incidental byproduct of the NVDA trim and cautious sizing -- it materially helped this week and should be a deliberate lever the routine can widen or narrow, not just something to note after the fact.

For commodity-linked equity positions (XLE), require the equity itself to confirm a technical breakout, not just the underlying commodity move, before adding to the position -- XLE has lagged a 20%+ Brent spike since the 07-23 entry, and Friday's sharp Brent pullback is the first real test of whether that lag resolves up or the entry was simply early.

Escalate the orphaned-branch/merge pipeline bug directly to the account owner as an infrastructure fix, not a recurring daily-routine patch: this is the fourth straight weekly review flagging it, two more sessions were stranded again this window (07-22, 07-23), and continued self-recovery risks a session eventually missing the recovery step and silently breaking the decision-continuity loop this whole system depends on.
summary: Second straight positive-alpha window: portfolio +0.74% vs SPY -0.39%, VT -0.19%, SSO -0.85% (2026-07-17 to 2026-07-24), with VT the best passive benchmark this week as broad markets slipped on Alphabet/Tesla capex-driven selloffs and new Section 301 tariffs. Two real trades this window: the 07-20 NVDA trim (15->11.4 sh, ~32.8%->24.9% of equity) finally executed a concentration-cap rule proactively rather than reactively -- the first time that pattern has generalized in the book's history -- and the same discipline extended to declining a fresh AAPL long candidate purely on pre-earnings-preview risk. A new XLE position (07-23, 7.7% of equity) on a confirmed Iran/Hormuz oil-escalation catalyst is directionally supported by the news (Brent topped $100/bbl) but hasn't been confirmed by XLE's own price yet, and Friday's sharp Brent pullback makes it a genuine 'too early' call rather than a settled win. The bigger structural story is that this week's alpha likely came more from a ~32% cash buffer cushioning Thursday's broad selloff than from stock-picking -- worth turning into a deliberate risk-budget rule rather than treating as incidental. SPY's stop cushion sat under 1% for five straight sessions without breaching or triggering any size reduction, a real near-miss on the account's largest, best-performing position. The orphaned-branch infrastructure bug persists for a fourth consecutive review and needs an owner-level fix, not further self-recovery.
